title: Hash Map
description: A hash map (dict in Python, map in Go) stores key-value pairs with average O(1) lookup, insert, and delete. It uses a hash function to compute an index into an array of buckets.
diagram:
Key  →  hash(key) % size  →  bucket index

  "apple" → 3 → [3]: ("apple", 5)
  "grape" → 7 → [7]: ("grape", 12)
  "pear"  → 3 → [3]: collision → chaining

Lookup:  O(1) avg,  O(n) worst
Insert:  O(1) avg
Delete:  O(1) avg
keyPoints: Average O(1) for get/set/delete
Collisions handled by chaining or open addressing
Load factor triggers resize (typically at 0.75)
Keys must be hashable (immutable in Python)
Common pattern: counting frequencies, caching, grouping

solutions.python:
# Frequency count
nums = [1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3]
freq = {}
for n in nums:
    freq[n] = freq.get(n, 0) + 1
# freq = {1:1, 2:2, 3:3}

# Using Counter
from collections import Counter
freq = Counter(nums)

# defaultdict
from collections import defaultdict
graph = defaultdict(list)
graph['a'].append('b')

title: Binary Search
description: Binary search finds a target in a sorted array in O(log n) time by repeatedly halving the search space. Key insight: if the middle element isn't the target, you can eliminate half the array.
diagram:
Array: [-1, 0, 3, 5, 9, 12]  target = 9
         0   1  2  3  4   5

Step 1:  L=0, R=5  mid=2  nums[2]=3 < 9  → L=3
Step 2:  L=3, R=5  mid=4  nums[4]=9 = 9  → return 4 ✓

Invariant: target is always in [L, R]
Loop condition: L <= R
keyPoints: Array MUST be sorted (or have a sorted property)
Use mid = left + (right - left) // 2 to avoid overflow
Three cases: found, go left (right = mid-1), go right (left = mid+1)
Template: while left <= right (inclusive bounds)
Common trick: binary search on the answer space, not just an array

title: Sliding Window
description: Sliding window maintains a window (subarray/substring) using two pointers and slides it across the input. Used for problems asking for optimal contiguous subarray/substring with some constraint.
diagram:
Find max sum subarray of size k=3:
[2, 1, 5, 1, 3, 2]

[2  1  5] 1  3  2   sum=8
 2 [1  5  1] 3  2   sum=7
 2  1 [5  1  3] 2   sum=9  ← max
 2  1  5 [1  3  2]  sum=6

Window slides: remove left, add right → O(n)
keyPoints: Fixed window: move both pointers together
Variable window: expand right, shrink left when constraint violated
Use for: max/min subarray, longest substring, anagram detection
Track window state with a counter/map/sum
Time: O(n) — each element enters and exits window once

solutions.python:
# Fixed window — max sum of size k
def max_sum_fixed(nums, k):
    window_sum = sum(nums[:k])
    best = window_sum
    for i in range(k, len(nums)):
        window_sum += nums[i] - nums[i - k]
        best = max(best, window_sum)
    return best

# Variable window — longest substring without repeating
def length_of_longest_substring(s):
    seen = set()
    left = best = 0
    for right, ch in enumerate(s):
        while ch in seen:
            seen.remove(s[left])
            left += 1
        seen.add(ch)
        best = max(best, right - left + 1)
    return best

title: DFS & BFS
description: DFS (Depth-First Search) explores as far as possible before backtracking — uses a stack (or recursion). BFS (Breadth-First Search) explores level by level — uses a queue. Both visit all nodes in O(V+E).
diagram:
Graph:
  1 → 2 → 4
  1 → 3 → 5

DFS (stack): 1 → 2 → 4 → 3 → 5  (go deep first)
BFS (queue): 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 5  (level by level)

When to use:
  DFS → path existence, cycles, topological sort
  BFS → shortest path (unweighted), level order
keyPoints: DFS: use recursion or explicit stack
BFS: always use a queue (deque)
Always track visited nodes to avoid infinite loops
BFS guarantees shortest path in unweighted graphs
DFS space: O(height), BFS space: O(width)

title: Two Pointers
description: Two pointers uses two indices that move through the data structure, often from both ends toward the middle or at different speeds. Reduces many O(n²) brute force problems to O(n).
diagram:
Opposite direction (sorted array sum):
Target = 9:  [1, 3, 5, 7, 9]
              L           R   sum=10 > 9 → R--
              L        R      sum=8  < 9 → L++
              L     R         sum=8  < 9 → L++
                 L  R         sum=10 > 9 → R--
                 LR           L==R → stop

Same direction (fast/slow — cycle detection):
  slow moves 1 step, fast moves 2 steps
keyPoints: Opposite ends: sort first, then converge (pair sum, palindrome check)
Same direction: one slow + one fast pointer (remove duplicates, cycle detection)
Requires sorted array for most opposite-direction patterns
Floyd's cycle detection: fast/slow pointers meet if there's a cycle
Time: O(n), Space: O(1)

solutions.python:
# Two Sum in sorted array
def two_sum_sorted(nums, target):
    left, right = 0, len(nums) - 1
    while left < right:
        s = nums[left] + nums[right]
        if s == target: return [left, right]
        elif s < target: left += 1
        else: right -= 1
    return []

# Remove duplicates in-place
def remove_duplicates(nums):
    k = 1
    for i in range(1, len(nums)):
        if nums[i] != nums[i-1]:
            nums[k] = nums[i]
            k += 1
    return k

# Detect cycle (Floyd's)
def has_cycle(head):
    slow = fast = head
    while fast and fast.next:
        slow = slow.next
        fast = fast.next.next
        if slow == fast: return True
    return False

title: Sets
description: A set stores unique elements with average O(1) add, remove, and lookup. No duplicates, no guaranteed order (except LinkedHashSet / sorted variants). Python has set, Go uses map[T]struct{}, Java has HashSet/TreeSet.
keyPoints: O(1) average for add / remove / contains
Go has no built-in set — use map[T]struct{} (zero memory value)
Python set elements must be hashable (no lists as elements)
Java HashSet = unordered, TreeSet = sorted O(log n)
Common use: deduplication, visited tracking, O(1) membership test

title: Rune & String Types
description: In Go, strings are immutable byte slices (UTF-8 encoded). A rune is an alias for int32 representing a Unicode code point. Iterating with range gives runes (correct for Unicode); indexing gives bytes. Python has no separate char type — strings are sequences of Unicode chars. Java uses char (UTF-16) and Character.
diagram:
Go string internals:
  s := "hello"
  s[0]         → byte  104  ('h')
  []rune(s)[0] → rune  104  ('h')

  s := "café"
  len(s)         → 5  (bytes, 'é' = 2 bytes)
  len([]rune(s)) → 4  (characters)

  range iterates runes:
  for i, r := range "café" { ... }
  // i=0 'c', i=1 'a', i=2 'f', i=3 'é'
keyPoints: Go: rune = int32, byte = uint8. Use rune for Unicode-safe char ops
Go: len(s) = byte count, len([]rune(s)) = character count
Go: range over string yields (byte index, rune) pairs
Python: str is Unicode by default, no separate char type. ord('a')=97, chr(97)='a'
Java: char is UTF-16 (2 bytes), Character has utility methods

solutions.python:
s = "hello"

# No char type — single char is a length-1 string
ch = s[0]          # 'h'
ord('h')           # 104  (Unicode code point)
chr(104)           # 'h'

# String → list of chars
chars = list(s)    # ['h','e','l','l','o']
''.join(chars)     # 'hello'

# Check char type
'a'.isalpha()      # True
'3'.isdigit()      # True
'a'.isupper()      # False
'A'.lower()        # 'a'

# Iterate
for ch in "café":
    print(ch)      # c a f é  (Unicode-safe)

title: Bit Manipulation
description: Bit manipulation operates directly on integer bits using bitwise operators. Essential for low-level optimization, flag masking, and many LeetCode problems. Works identically in Python, Go, and Java.
diagram:
Operators (integer level):
  a & b   AND   — 1 only if both 1
  a | b   OR    — 1 if either is 1
  a ^ b   XOR   — 1 if bits differ
  ~a      NOT   — flip all bits
  a << n  Left  — multiply by 2^n
  a >> n  Right — divide by 2^n

Common masks (for bit i):
  mask = 1 << i
  Get i-th:    (n >> i) & 1
  Set i-th:    n | mask
  Clear i-th:  n & ~mask
  Toggle i-th: n ^ mask
keyPoints: x & (x-1)  removes the lowest set bit — use to count set bits
x & (-x) isolates the lowest set bit
x ^ x = 0  and  x ^ 0 = x — XOR to find unpaired element
Left shift << n  =  multiply by 2^n
Right shift >> n  =  divide by 2^n (arithmetic in Java/Go, logical in Python)

solutions.python:
n = 0b1011   # 11 in binary

# Operators
print(n & 0b1010)   # AND  → 0b1010 = 10
print(n | 0b0100)   # OR   → 0b1111 = 15
print(n ^ 0b1001)   # XOR  → 0b0010 = 2
print(~n)           # NOT  → -12 (two's complement)
print(n << 1)       # Left shift  → 22
print(n >> 1)       # Right shift → 5

i = 2  # operate on bit 2
mask = 1 << i

# Get i-th bit
(n >> i) & 1          # 0 or 1

# Set i-th bit (force to 1)
n | mask

# Clear i-th bit (force to 0)
n & ~mask

# Toggle i-th bit (flip)
n ^ mask

# Count set bits
bin(n).count('1')     # Pythonic
int.bit_count(n)      # Python 3.10+

# Check power of 2
n > 0 and (n & (n-1)) == 0

# Classic XOR trick: find single unpaired number
nums = [1, 2, 2, 3, 3]
result = 0
for x in nums: result ^= x
# result == 1
